title: The art of waiting in line gracefully
summary: Argue what it means to wait in line well, and why the quality of waiting reveals something important about character and community.
topic: The art of waiting in line gracefully
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (350–500 words) arguing what makes someone good at waiting in line, and what their behaviour reveals about them. Describe a specific waiting situation you have witnessed. Use present perfect, modal verbs, and at least one concession with 'although' or 'however'.
hintStarters: Although waiting in line seems like a trivial act, I have come to believe that…
I have watched hundreds of people in queues, and the ones who do it well all seem to share…
If queuing were taught as a social skill, the lesson would probably start not with patience but with…

hintVocab.meaningVi: xếp hàng một cách thanh thản
biểu lộ sự thiếu kiên nhẫn
giữ vị trí một cách lặng lẽ
đối xử với người khác như người ngang hàng
làm tan biến sự căng thẳng
một hành động tôn trọng nhỏ bé
làm cho việc chờ đợi trở nên chịu được

sampleText:
I have stood in many queues in my life, but the one I remember most clearly was at a pharmacy in Montreal during a heavy snowstorm. The line stretched back to the door and had been standing still for nearly twenty minutes. A man near the front, around sixty years old, had noticed that the person directly behind him was holding a small child who was crying. Without saying anything dramatic, he simply gestured for them to move ahead of him. The child stopped crying almost immediately, the mother looked relieved, and the rest of the line seemed to exhale. Nothing else changed — they all still had to wait — but the quality of the wait had shifted entirely.

Although waiting in line seems like a trivial act, I have come to believe it is one of the small tests of social character. The people who do it well share a few recognisable qualities. They hold their ground without pushing. They do not signal their impatience through sighs, phone checks, or pointed looks at whoever is serving. They keep their composure even when the person ahead of them is slow, and they make space for others without needing to be asked. These are not qualities that require great sacrifice; they are the small acts of respect that make shared discomfort more bearable for everyone. A queue is a social contract in miniature — everyone agrees to take turns and treat each other as equals — and the people who honour it quietly are, I would argue, some of the most dependable people in a society.

However, queuing well is harder than it looks, and not every situation makes it easy. Long waits in uncomfortable conditions can wear down even the most patient people. I would not condemn someone who sighs after forty-five minutes standing in the cold. What matters is the general direction of your behaviour — whether you tend to make the situation slightly better or slightly worse for the people around you. The man in Montreal had not planned his gesture; he had simply developed a habit of noticing other people. If queuing were taught as a social skill, the lesson would start not with patience but with attention: the habit of scanning a room and asking, quietly, whether someone nearby might need the space you are standing in.

Không nói điều gì kịch tính

sự khó chịu chung dễ chịu hơn

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Past perfect for sequence in narrative
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'The line stretched back to the door and had been standing still for nearly twenty minutes' — quá khứ hoàn thành tiếp diễn ('had been standing') nhấn mạnh rằng hàng người đã đứng đó trong một khoảng thời gian liên tục trước thời điểm được nhắc đến trong câu chuyện.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'The line stretched back to the door and had been standing still for nearly twenty minutes' — the past perfect continuous ('had been standing') emphasises that the queue had been there for a continuous period before the moment described in the story.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Concession with 'although'
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Although waiting in line seems like a trivial act, I have come to believe it is one of the small tests of social character' dùng 'although' để thừa nhận quan điểm thông thường (chờ hàng là chuyện nhỏ nhặt) trước khi đề xuất ý nghĩa sâu xa hơn.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Although waiting in line seems like a trivial act, I have come to believe it is one of the small tests of social character' uses 'although' to acknowledge the common view (queuing is trivial) before proposing a deeper significance.

sampleTranslationVi:
Tôi đã đứng trong nhiều hàng chờ trong cuộc đời mình, nhưng cái tôi nhớ rõ nhất là ở một hiệu thuốc ở Montreal trong một trận bão tuyết lớn. Hàng người kéo dài đến tận cửa và đã đứng yên gần hai mươi phút. Một người đàn ông gần phía trước, khoảng sáu mươi tuổi, nhận ra rằng người ngay phía sau ông đang bế một đứa trẻ nhỏ đang khóc. Không nói điều gì kịch tính, ông chỉ đơn giản ra hiệu cho họ đi lên trước. Đứa trẻ gần như ngừng khóc ngay lập tức, người mẹ trông nhẹ nhõm, và phần còn lại của hàng người dường như thở phào. Không có gì khác thay đổi — họ vẫn phải chờ đợi — nhưng chất lượng của sự chờ đợi đã thay đổi hoàn toàn.

Mặc dù chờ hàng có vẻ như là một hành động tầm thường, tôi đã đi đến tin rằng đó là một trong những bài kiểm tra nhỏ về tính cách xã hội. Những người làm điều đó tốt đều có một vài phẩm chất dễ nhận ra. Họ giữ vị trí mà không xô đẩy. Họ không biểu lộ sự thiếu kiên nhẫn qua tiếng thở dài, nhìn điện thoại, hay những cái nhìn có chủ ý vào người đang phục vụ. Họ giữ bình tĩnh ngay cả khi người phía trước họ chậm chạp, và họ nhường chỗ cho người khác mà không cần được yêu cầu. Đây không phải là những phẩm chất đòi hỏi sự hy sinh lớn lao; chúng là những hành động tôn trọng nhỏ bé làm cho sự khó chịu chung dễ chịu hơn cho mọi người. Một hàng chờ là một khế ước xã hội thu nhỏ — mọi người đồng ý thay phiên nhau và đối xử với nhau như người ngang hàng — và những người tuân thủ nó một cách lặng lẽ là, tôi muốn lập luận, những người đáng tin cậy nhất trong xã hội.

Tuy nhiên, xếp hàng tốt khó hơn vẻ ngoài, và không phải mọi tình huống đều dễ dàng. Những lần chờ lâu trong điều kiện khó chịu có thể làm mòn ngay cả những người kiên nhẫn nhất. Tôi sẽ không phán xét ai đó thở dài sau bốn mươi lăm phút đứng trong trời lạnh. Điều quan trọng là xu hướng chung trong hành vi của bạn — liệu bạn có xu hướng làm cho tình huống tốt hơn hay tệ hơn một chút cho những người xung quanh. Người đàn ông ở Montreal đã không lên kế hoạch cho cử chỉ của mình; ông chỉ đơn giản là đã phát triển thói quen chú ý đến người khác. Nếu xếp hàng được dạy như một kỹ năng xã hội, bài học sẽ bắt đầu không phải với sự kiên nhẫn mà với sự chú ý: thói quen quan sát khắp một không gian và tự hỏi, một cách lặng lẽ, liệu có ai gần đó có thể cần không gian mà bạn đang đứng không.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the past perfect continuous?
options: The line has been standing still for nearly twenty minutes when he arrived.
The line had been standing still for nearly twenty minutes when he arrived.
The line had standing still for nearly twenty minutes when he arrived.
The line have been standing still for nearly twenty minutes when he arrived.
mcQuestions.explanation: Past perfect continuous = 'had been + -ing'. It describes an ongoing past action before another past event.
mcQuestions.hint: Past perfect continuous = 'had been + verb-ing' for a continuous action before a past point.

options: Although waiting in line seems like a trivial act, but I believe it is a test of social character.
Waiting in line seems like a trivial act, although because I believe it is a test of social character.
Although waiting in line seems like a trivial act, I believe it is a test of social character.
Although, waiting in line seems like a trivial act, I believe it is a test of social character.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + clause, main clause' — no comma after 'although' and no extra conjunction like 'but'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Although' provides the contrast alone; don't add 'but' or put a comma after 'although'.

options: However queuing well is harder than it looks, and not every situation makes it easy.
Queuing well is harder than it looks, however it is not every situation that makes it easy.
Queuing well is harder than it looks however, not every situation makes it easy.
However, queuing well is harder than it looks, and not every situation makes it easy.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'However' starting a sentence is followed by a comma. Mid-sentence placement needs commas on both sides.
mcQuestions.hint: At the start of a sentence, 'however' must be immediately followed by a comma.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses a second conditional?
options: If queuing were taught as a social skill, the lesson would start with attention.
If queuing was taught as a social skill, the lesson will start with attention.
If queuing were taught as a social skill, the lesson would started with attention.
If queuing were taught as a social skill, the lesson would to start with attention.
mcQuestions.explanation: Second conditional with subjunctive 'were': 'if + were + past participle, would + bare infinitive'.
mcQuestions.hint: Formal second conditional uses 'if + were + past participle' + 'would + bare infinitive'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which modal verb phrase correctly expresses an unwillingness to judge?
options: I will not condemn someone who sighs after forty-five minutes in the cold.
I would not condemn someone who sighs after forty-five minutes in the cold.
I would not to condemn someone who sighs after forty-five minutes in the cold.
I would not condemning someone who sighs after forty-five minutes in the cold.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Would not + bare infinitive' expresses a strong personal stance; 'will not' is more factual/predictive.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Would not + bare infinitive' — modal negation uses the bare infinitive, never 'to' or '-ing'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the present perfect correctly to describe a personal development?
options: I came to believe that queuing is a test of social character.
I have come to believing that queuing is a test of social character.
I have come to believe that queuing is a test of social character.
I have came to believe that queuing is a test of social character.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Have come to believe' (present perfect + infinitive) describes a gradual change in belief up to now.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Come to + infinitive' describes a gradual realisation; present perfect shows it is relevant now.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which relative clause is correctly formed?
options: A person who they hold their ground without pushing and make space for others without being asked.
A person that holds their ground without pushing and who makes space for others without asked.
A person who holds their ground without pushing and making space for others without to be asked.
A person who holds their ground without pushing and makes space for others without being asked.
mcQuestions.explanation: Subject relative clause 'who holds … and makes' uses parallel present simple verbs; no pronoun repetition.
mcQuestions.hint: Don't repeat the subject ('who they'); both verbs in a joined relative clause share the same form.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses parallel structure correctly in a list of qualities?
options: They hold their ground, keep their composure, and make space for others.
They hold their ground, keeping their composure, and make space for others.
They hold their ground, keep their composure, and making space for others.
They hold their ground, to keep their composure, and make space for others.
mcQuestions.explanation: All three verbs after 'they' must be in the same form: present simple 'hold … keep … make'.
mcQuestions.hint: In a list after the same subject, all verbs must share the same tense and form.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'whether' correctly in an indirect question?
options: Ask yourself whether someone nearby might needs the space you are standing in.
Ask yourself whether someone nearby might need the space you are standing in.
Ask yourself if whether someone nearby might need the space you are standing in.
Ask yourself whether does someone nearby need the space you are standing in.
mcQuestions.explanation: In indirect questions with 'whether', use statement word order and the bare infinitive after modal 'might'.
mcQuestions.hint: Indirect question: 'whether + subject + modal + bare infinitive' (no inversion, no auxiliary).

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses 'tend to' correctly to describe a habitual behaviour?
options: Whether you tend making the situation slightly better or slightly worse for those around you.
Whether you tend to making the situation slightly better or slightly worse for those around you.
Whether you tend to make the situation slightly better or slightly worse for those around you.
Whether you tends to make the situation slightly better or slightly worse for those around you.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Tend to + bare infinitive' is the correct structure; second person singular takes 'tend' without -s.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Tend to' is always followed by the bare infinitive; second person 'you' never adds -s.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'whereas' correctly to contrast two types of behaviour?
options: Some people make queues better, whereas but others make them significantly worse.
Some people make queues better whereas, others make them significantly worse.
Whereas some people make queues better others make them significantly worse.
Some people make queues better, whereas others make them significantly worse.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Whereas' mid-sentence (after a comma) contrasts two clauses; no extra conjunction and no comma after 'whereas'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Whereas' as a mid-sentence connector: place a comma before it, none after; no 'but' needed.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Are there cultures or countries where queuing is rarely practised, but social order in crowds is maintained in other ways? What does this suggest about the relationship between rules and behaviour?
